The most remarkable thing about the old world is its destruction by a flood. We are told about the abundant wickedness of that wicked world, God's just anger, and his determination to punish it. In all ages, there has been a particular curse from God on marriages between people who profess true religion and those who openly oppose it. The bad example of ungodly people corrupts or greatly harms the other party. Family religion is ended, and children are raised according to the worldly values of that parent who does not fear God. If we claim to be the sons and daughters of the Lord Almighty, we must not marry without his consent. He will never give his blessing if we prefer beauty, intelligence, wealth, or worldly honors over faith and holiness. The Spirit of God tried to influence people by sending Enoch, Noah, and perhaps others to preach to them; by being willing to be gracious despite their rebellions; and by creating alarm and conviction in their consciences. But the Lord declared that his Spirit would not always try people this way; he would leave them to become hardened in sin and ready for destruction. He decided this because humans are flesh: not only weak and frail, but also carnal and depraved, having misused the noble abilities of their souls to satisfy their corrupt desires. God sees all the wickedness among humanity; it cannot be hidden from him now; and if it is not repented of, it will be made known by him soon. The wickedness of a people is great when notorious sinners are respected among them. Much sin was committed everywhere, by every kind of person. Anyone could see that human wickedness was widespread: but God saw that every plan or purpose in the thoughts of humanity’s heart was only evil continually. This was the bitter root, the corrupt source. The heart was deceitful and desperately wicked; the principles were corrupt; the habits and dispositions were evil. Their plans and schemes were wicked. They did evil deliberately, plotting how to cause harm. There was no good among them. God saw humanity’s wickedness as an injury and a wrong. He saw it as a tender father sees the foolishness and stubbornness of a rebellious and disobedient child, which grieves him and makes him wish he had not had children. The words used here are remarkable; they are used in the way humans speak, and do not mean that God can change or be unhappy. Does God hate our sin like this? And should we not be deeply saddened by it? Oh, may we look on Him whom we have grieved, and mourn! God regretted that he had made humanity; but we never find him regretting that he redeemed humanity. God resolved to destroy humanity: the original word is very striking, ‘I will wipe off humanity from the earth,’ as dirt or filth is wiped off from a place which should be clean, and is thrown to the dunghill, the proper place for it. God speaks of humanity as his own creation when he resolves upon their punishment. Those forfeit their lives who do not fulfill the purpose of their existence. God spoke of his resolution concerning people after his Spirit had long tried them in vain. No one is punished by the justice of God, but those who hate to be reformed by the grace of God.

Noah did not find favor in the eyes of men; they hated and persecuted him because both his life and preaching condemned the world: but he found grace in the eyes of the Lord, and this made him more truly honorable than renowned people. Let this be our chief desire, let us labor that we may be accepted by him. When the rest of the world was wicked, Noah maintained his integrity. God’s goodwill toward Noah produced this good work in him. He was a just man, that is, justified before God, by faith in the promised Seed. As such he was made holy, and had right principles; and was righteous in his conduct. He was not only honest, but devout; it was his constant care to do the will of God. God looks down upon those with an eye of favor who sincerely look up to him with an eye of faith. It is easy to be religious when religion is fashionable; but it shows strong faith and resolution to go against the current, and to stand for God when no one else stands for him; Noah did so. All kinds of sin were found among people. They corrupted God's worship. Sin fills the earth with violence, and this fully justified God’s resolution to destroy the world. The contagion spread. When wickedness becomes widespread, ruin is not far off; while there are still some praying people in a nation, to prevent it from overflowing, judgments may be kept away for a long time; but when everyone works to tear down the defenses by sin, and no one stands in the gap to repair the breach, what can be expected but a flood of wrath?

God told Noah his purpose to destroy the wicked world by water. The secret of the Lord is with those who fear him. It is with all believers, enabling them to understand and apply the declarations and warnings of the written word. God chose to do it by a flood of waters, which would drown the world. As he chooses the rod with which he corrects his children, so he chooses the sword with which he cuts off his enemies. God established his covenant with Noah. This is the first place in the Bible where the word ‘covenant’ is found; it seems to mean, 1. The covenant of providence; that the course of nature shall be continued to the end of time. 2. The covenant of grace; that God would be a God to Noah, and that out of his seed God would take to himself a people. God directed Noah to make an ark. This ark was like the hull of a ship, fitted to float upon the waters. It was very large, half the size of St. Paul's cathedral, and would hold more than eighteen of the largest ships now used. God could have secured Noah without putting him to any care, or pains, or trouble, but employed him in making that which was to be the means to preserve him, for the trial of his faith and obedience. Both the providence of God, and the grace of God, own and crown the obedient and diligent. God gave Noah particular orders how to make the ark, which could not therefore but be well fitted for the purpose. God promised Noah that he and his family should be kept alive in the ark. What we do in obedience to God, we and our families are likely to benefit from. The piety of parents benefits their children in this life, and furthers them on the way to eternal life, if they improve it.

Noah's faith triumphed over all corrupt reasonings. To build such a large structure, one like which he had never seen, and to provide food for the living creatures, would require from him great care, labor, and expense. His neighbors would laugh at him. But Noah overcame all such objections by faith; his obedience was ready and resolute. Having begun to build, he did not stop until he had finished: so did he, and so must we do. He feared the flood, and therefore prepared the ark. And in the warning given to Noah, there is a more solemn warning given to us, to flee from the coming wrath, which will sweep unbelievers into destruction. Christ, the true Noah, who will comfort us, has by his sufferings already prepared the ark, and kindly invites us by faith to enter in. While the day of his patience continues, let us hear and obey his voice.
